title: Implement KiCad PCB export integration
description:
Main export function that integrates segment/via generation with KiCad file writing.

## Implementation

```python
def export_routed_pcb(
    base_pcb_path: str,
    routes: dict[str, RoutePath],
    output_path: str,
    trace_width: float = 0.25,
    via_size: float = 0.8,
    via_drill: float = 0.4,
):
    """Export routes to KiCad PCB file."""
    # Parse base PCB
    pcb = parse_kicad_pcb(base_pcb_path)
    
    # Get grid parameters from routing context
    origin = pcb.board_origin
    cell_size = 0.5  # or from config
    layer_map = {0: 'F.Cu', 1: 'B.Cu'}
    
    # Generate geometry from routes
    all_segments = []
    all_vias = []
    
    for net_name, path in routes.items():
        if path.success:
            all_segments.extend(path_to_segments(path, trace_width, layer_map, origin, cell_size))
            all_vias.extend(path_to_vias(path, via_size, via_drill, origin, cell_size))
    
    # Merge into PCB and write
    pcb.add_segments(all_segments)
    pcb.add_vias(all_vias)
    pcb.write(output_path)
```

## KiCad Parser Extensions Required

title: Epic: Hardware Safety Interlock Design and Verification
description:
Design and verify all hardware safety interlocks required for safe induction cooker operation. This epic MUST be completed before any power stage testing.

SAFETY PHILOSOPHY:
Hardware interlocks provide fail-safe protection independent of firmware. Even if the ESP32 crashes, locks up, or has a bug, hardware interlocks must disable the power stage within <1µs.

REQUIRED INTERLOCKS:
1. Overcurrent Protection (OCP) - Trip at 50A peak (125% of rated)
2. Overvoltage Protection (OVP) - Trip at 400V DC bus (110% of nominal)
3. Undervoltage Lockout (UVLO) - Already in UCC21550B, verified in temper-8l2
4. Gate Driver Fault - Fast disable path from comparator to UCC21550 DISABLE pin
5. Thermal Shutdown - Hardware comparator on IGBT heatsink NTC

RESPONSE TIME REQUIREMENT:
- OCP: <1µs from detection to gate driver disable
- OVP: <10µs (slower, bus capacitor provides some hold time)
- Thermal: <100ms (thermal mass provides time)

DESIGN APPROACH:
- Use fast comparators (LM339 or TLV3202 for speed)
- Direct hardware path to UCC21550 DISABLE pin
- OR-gate combining all fault sources
- Latching behavior (requires manual reset after fault)
- LED indicators for fault diagnosis

DELIVERABLES:
1. sim_17_ocp_protection.cir - OCP circuit simulation
2. sim_18_ovp_protection.cir - OVP circuit simulation
3. sim_19_thermal_shutdown.cir - NTC comparator simulation
4. sim_20_interlock_integration.cir - Combined fault logic
5. SAFETY_INTERLOCK_DESIGN.md - Complete design document

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- All simulations pass with documented response times
- Response times meet requirements with 2x margin
- Fault conditions tested: overcurrent, overvoltage, overtemp, gate driver UVLO
- Reset and indication logic verified

BLOCKS:
- temper-gqb (Half-Bridge Power Stage) - Cannot test safely without interlocks
- temper-axx (Resonant Tank) - Cannot test safely without interlocks

title: Create current transformer SPICE model
description:
Create a SPICE subcircuit model for the current transformer used in tank current sensing and OCP.

CT REQUIREMENTS:
- Primary: 1 turn (tank wire passes through CT core)
- Secondary: 1000 turns (typical, gives 1000:1 ratio)
- Rated current: 50A primary → 50mA secondary
- Frequency range: 20kHz - 100kHz (induction cooker switching range)
- Accuracy: ±1% for power measurement, ±10% for OCP (conservative trip)

CT MODEL PARAMETERS:
- Turns ratio: N = 1000:1
- Magnetizing inductance: Lm = 10mH (typical for ferrite core)
- Leakage inductance: Ll = 100µH (<<Lm)
- Winding resistance: Rw = 50Ω (secondary)

DELIVERABLES:
- simulation/models/current_transformer.sub
- Validation tests: DC rejection, frequency response, phase accuracy

title: Design overcurrent protection (OCP) comparator circuit
description:
Design hardware OCP circuit using current transformer output to detect overcurrent and disable gate drivers within <1µs.

CIRCUIT REQUIREMENTS:
- Input: CT secondary voltage (proportional to tank current)
- Threshold: 50A peak (set by voltage divider on comparator reference)
- Output: Logic-level signal to fault OR-gate
- Response time: <500ns comparator + <500ns gate driver disable = <1µs total

DESIGN APPROACH:
1. CT burden resistor sized for 50A → ~2.5V
2. Fast comparator (TLV3202: 40ns propagation)
3. Reference voltage from resistor divider
4. Hysteresis resistor to prevent chatter
5. Open-collector output for OR-gate

SIMULATION: sim_17_ocp_protection.cir
DELIVERABLES: OCP circuit, component selection, trip threshold calculation

title: Design overvoltage protection (OVP) comparator circuit
description:
Design hardware OVP circuit to detect DC bus overvoltage (>400V) and disable gate drivers.

CIRCUIT REQUIREMENTS:
- Input: Isolated DC bus voltage sense (resistive divider + optocoupler)
- Threshold: 400V DC (trip point), 380V reset (hysteresis)
- Output: Logic-level signal to fault OR-gate
- Response time: <10µs

ISOLATION APPROACH:
- High-value resistive divider (3x 1MΩ) + optocoupler for isolation
- Divider: 310V → 3.1V using 3MΩ/30kΩ (100:1 ratio)
- Comparator on isolated side for clean logic output

SIMULATION: sim_18_ovp_protection.cir
DELIVERABLES: OVP circuit, isolation boundary documentation

title: Design thermal shutdown comparator circuit
description:
Design hardware thermal protection using NTC thermistor on IGBT heatsink.

CIRCUIT REQUIREMENTS:
- Input: NTC thermistor (10kΩ @ 25°C typical) on IGBT heatsink
- Trip threshold: 85°C (conservative, IGBT rated to 150°C Tj)
- Reset threshold: 75°C (hysteresis prevents cycling)
- Response time: <100ms (thermal mass provides time)

NTC BEHAVIOR:
- At 25°C: 10kΩ, At 85°C: ~2.5kΩ
- Voltage divider with pullup resistor
- Comparator trips when voltage falls below threshold

SIMULATION: sim_19_thermal_shutdown.cir
DELIVERABLES: Thermal circuit, NTC selection, calibration procedure

title: Design fault integration OR-gate and latching logic
description:
Design the fault combination and latching logic that feeds the UCC21550 DISABLE pin.

CIRCUIT REQUIREMENTS:
- Inputs: OCP_FAULT, OVP_FAULT, THERMAL_FAULT, (optional: ESP32_WDT_FAULT)
- Output: GATE_DISABLE signal to UCC21550 DISABLE pin (active HIGH disables)
- Behavior: Latching (any fault latches output HIGH until manual reset)
- Indication: LED per fault source for diagnosis

LATCHING REQUIREMENT:
Once a fault occurs, the system must remain disabled until:
1. Fault condition clears
2. User presses RESET button

IMPLEMENTATION: Comparator with positive feedback (self-latching)

DELIVERABLES: Fault logic schematic, timing diagram, LED indication

title: Expand PCB Test Suite to 25 Real-World Projects
description:
Expand the external PCB test suite from 5 to 25 projects to provide comprehensive validation coverage for temper-placer optimizer.

## Current Status
- **Existing**: 5 KiCad 6 projects (piantor_left/right, bitaxe_ultra, libresolar_bms, rp2040_designguide)
- **Target**: 25 total projects (20 additional)
- **Coverage**: Much better statistical validation of optimizer quality

## PCB Selection Criteria
1. **License**: Permissive open-source (Apache-2.0, MIT, CERN-OHL-S, etc.)
2. **Format**: KiCad 6 native files (version 20220714+)
3. **Complexity**: Mix of small (10-50), medium (50-150), large (150-300) component counts
4. **Domains**: Diverse application areas (IoT, power electronics, motor control, audio, RF, etc.)
5. **Quality**: Production hardware, not tutorials or experiments
6. **Source**: Established open-source hardware projects

## Target Project Categories
- **Power Electronics**: Motor drivers, buck/boost converters, battery management
- **IoT/Embedded**: ESP32, STM32, Arduino-compatible boards
- **Audio**: Audio interfaces, amplifiers, synthesizers
- **RF/Wireless**: WiFi modules, radio transceivers, antenna designs
- **Industrial**: PLC modules, sensor interfaces, control systems
- **Consumer**: Gaming peripherals, LED controllers, USB devices

## Implementation Steps
1. **Research Phase**: Identify 30-40 candidate projects from GitHub
2. **Screening**: Verify KiCad 6 format, license compatibility, component counts
3. **Download**: Clone repositories and extract PCB files
4. **Processing**: Generate unrouted versions and baseline metrics
5. **Validation**: Ensure all 25 projects parse correctly and have baselines
6. **Testing**: Run optimizer on subset to verify functionality

## Success Criteria
- **25 projects total** with complete test fixtures
- **Balanced complexity**: ~8 small, ~12 medium, ~5 large projects
- **Diverse domains**: At least 6 different application categories
- **All projects pass**: Parser validation and baseline extraction
- **Test coverage**: Optimizer runs successfully on all projects
- **Performance**: Total test suite completes in <30 minutes

## Files to Update
-  - Add new project definitions
-  - Handle larger batch downloads
-  - Update project list
- Documentation: Update test coverage metrics

## Dependencies
- temper-fqt.4 (placement comparison framework) - ✅ COMPLETE
- Existing PCB parsing and baseline extraction pipeline - ✅ COMPLETE

## Estimated Effort
- **Research**: 2-3 hours to identify and screen candidates
- **Download/Processing**: 1-2 hours for automated processing
- **Validation**: 1 hour to verify all projects work
- **Total**: 4-6 hours focused work

## Impact
- **Statistical Significance**: 5x more data points for optimizer validation
- **Real-world Coverage**: Test on diverse PCB design patterns and constraints
- **Quality Assurance**: Better detection of edge cases and failure modes
- **Benchmarking**: Establish robust performance baselines across project types

All projects use KiCad 6+ format for compatibility with kiutils. The manifest includes proper categorization, complexity estimates, and comprehensive descriptions for each project.

title: Formal Escape Routing Phase for SMD/BGA Components
description: Implement a formal escape routing phase that enables routing of real-world SMD chips (QFP, QFN, BGA). The current router treats all pins equally, causing center pins in dense grids to be topologically trapped. This epic adds a pre-routing escape phase that generates dog-bone fanouts in concentric rings, enabling the main router to work from component edges rather than trapped centers.

title: Implement Pin Ring Classifier

title: Implement Escape Direction Assignment

title: CLI Integration for Deterministic Placement
description:
## Overview
Add `place-deterministic` CLI command to invoke the new deterministic placement pipeline.

## Scope
- New command: `temper-placer place-deterministic`
- Config options for each phase
- Progress reporting
- Output format matching existing `place` command

## Usage

```bash
# Basic usage
temper-placer place-deterministic input.kicad_pcb -c constraints.yaml -o output.kicad_pcb

# With options
temper-placer place-deterministic input.kicad_pcb \
  --max-iterations 5 \
  --routability-threshold 0.85 \
  --max-movement 3.0 \
  --no-local-refinement \
  -o output.kicad_pcb
```

## TDD Requirements
- Test CLI parses arguments correctly
- Test invokes DeterministicPlacer
- Test output file written
- Test error reporting

## Key Files
- `src/temper_placer/cli/__init__.py`
- `tests/cli/test_place_deterministic.py` (new)

## Success Criteria
- Command available and documented
- All options work correctly
- Progress shown during placement
- Matches existing CLI patterns

title: place-deterministic CLI command
description:
## Overview
Add `place-deterministic` command to CLI that invokes the `DeterministicPlacer`.

## TDD Approach (MANDATORY)

```python
# Test 1: Command exists and has correct options
def test_place_deterministic_command_exists():
    from click.testing import CliRunner
    from temper_placer.cli import cli
    
    runner = CliRunner()
    result = runner.invoke(cli, ['place-deterministic', '--help'])
    
    assert result.exit_code == 0
    assert '--max-iterations' in result.output
    assert '--routability-threshold' in result.output
    assert '--max-movement' in result.output
    assert '--no-local-refinement' in result.output

# Test 2: Basic invocation works
def test_place_deterministic_basic_invocation(tmp_path):
    runner = CliRunner()
    input_pcb = tmp_path / 'input.kicad_pcb'
    output_pcb = tmp_path / 'output.kicad_pcb'
    constraints = tmp_path / 'constraints.yaml'
    
    # Create minimal test files
    input_pcb.write_text(MINIMAL_KICAD_PCB)
    constraints.write_text(MINIMAL_CONSTRAINTS)
    
    result = runner.invoke(cli, [
        'place-deterministic',
        str(input_pcb),
        '-c', str(constraints),
        '-o', str(output_pcb),
    ])
    
    assert result.exit_code == 0
    assert output_pcb.exists()

# Test 3: Options passed to placer
def test_place_deterministic_options_passed(mocker):
    mock_placer = mocker.patch('temper_placer.cli.DeterministicPlacer')
    
    runner = CliRunner()
    result = runner.invoke(cli, [
        'place-deterministic',
        'input.kicad_pcb',
        '--max-iterations', '5',
        '--routability-threshold', '0.85',
        '--max-movement', '3.0',
        '--no-local-refinement',
    ])
    
    config = mock_placer.return_value.place.call_args[0][3]
    assert config.max_routability_iterations == 5
    assert config.routability_threshold == 0.85
    assert config.max_movement_mm == 3.0
    assert config.enable_local_refinement == False

# Test 4: Error handling
def test_place_deterministic_reports_preflight_errors(tmp_path):
    runner = CliRunner()
    
    # Constraints with impossible proximity
    constraints = tmp_path / 'bad_constraints.yaml'
    constraints.write_text('''
groups:
  - name: impossible
    components: [IC1, C1]
    proximity:
      - pair: [IC1, C1]
        max_distance_mm: 1.0  # Impossible
''')
    
    result = runner.invoke(cli, [
        'place-deterministic',
        str(input_pcb),
        '-c', str(constraints),
    ])
    
    assert result.exit_code != 0
    assert 'PROXIMITY_INFEASIBLE' in result.output or 'infeasible' in result.output.lower()

# Test 5: Progress output
def test_place_deterministic_shows_progress(tmp_path):
    runner = CliRunner()
    
    result = runner.invoke(cli, [
        'place-deterministic',
        str(input_pcb),
        '-c', str(constraints),
        '-o', str(output_pcb),
        '--verbose',
    ])
    
    assert 'Preflight' in result.output or 'Step 0' in result.output
    assert 'Template' in result.output or 'Step 1' in result.output
```

## Implementation

```python
@cli.command('place-deterministic')
@click.argument('input_pcb', type=click.Path(exists=True))
@click.option('-c', '--constraints', type=click.Path(exists=True), required=True)
@click.option('-o', '--output', type=click.Path(), required=True)
@click.option('--max-iterations', type=int, default=3, help='Max routability iterations')
@click.option('--routability-threshold', type=float, default=0.90, help='Min routing completion rate')
@click.option('--max-movement', type=float, default=2.0, help='Max local refinement movement (mm)')
@click.option('--no-local-refinement', is_flag=True, help='Skip local gradient refinement')
@click.option('--critical-nets', multiple=True, help='Nets to prioritize for routing')
@click.option('-v', '--verbose', is_flag=True, help='Show detailed progress')
def place_deterministic(
    input_pcb, constraints, output,
    max_iterations, routability_threshold, max_movement,
    no_local_refinement, critical_nets, verbose
):
    '''Deterministic template-based placement with routability feedback.'''
    
    # Load inputs
    if verbose:
        click.echo('Loading PCB and constraints...')
    
    netlist, board = load_kicad_pcb(input_pcb)
    placement_constraints = load_constraints(constraints, board)
    
    # Configure placer
    config = DeterministicPlacerConfig(
        max_routability_iterations=max_iterations,
        routability_threshold=routability_threshold,
        critical_nets=list(critical_nets),
        enable_local_refinement=not no_local_refinement,
    )
    config.local_refinement_config = LocalRefinementConfig(
        max_movement_mm=max_movement,
    )
    
    # Run placement
    placer = DeterministicPlacer()
    
    with click.progressbar(length=5, label='Placing') as bar:
        def progress_callback(step, message):
            if verbose:
                click.echo(f'  {step}: {message}')
            bar.update(1)
        
        result = placer.place(
            netlist, board, placement_constraints, config,
            progress_callback=progress_callback
        )
    
    if not result.success:
        click.echo(click.style('Placement failed:', fg='red'))
        for error in result.errors:
            click.echo(f'  [{error.code}] {error.message}')
            if error.suggestion:
                click.echo(f'    Suggestion: {error.suggestion}')
        raise SystemExit(1)
    
    # Report results
    click.echo(f'Placement complete in {result.iterations_used} iteration(s)')
    if result.routing_result:
        click.echo(f'Routing completion: {result.routing_result.completion_rate:.1%}')
    
    # Write output
    write_kicad_pcb(output, netlist, result.positions, board)
    click.echo(f'Output written to {output}')
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Tests written FIRST
- [ ] Command registered in CLI
- [ ] All options work correctly
- [ ] Progress bar shown during placement
- [ ] Verbose mode shows step details
- [ ] Errors reported with suggestions
- [ ] Exit code non-zero on failure

title: End-to-end integration tests
description:
## Overview
Create comprehensive end-to-end tests validating the full deterministic placement pipeline.

## TDD Approach (MANDATORY)

```python
# Test 1: Full pipeline on Temper board
def test_deterministic_pipeline_on_temper_board():
    '''End-to-end test with actual Temper board constraints.'''
    netlist = load_temper_netlist()
    board = load_temper_board()
    constraints = load_temper_constraints()
    
    placer = DeterministicPlacer()
    config = DeterministicPlacerConfig()
    
    result = placer.place(netlist, board, constraints, config)
    
    assert result.success
    assert result.routing_result.completion_rate >= 0.85
    
    # Validate no overlaps
    assert not has_overlaps(result.positions, netlist)
    
    # Validate zone compliance
    for comp in netlist.components:
        if comp.zone:
            zone = board.get_zone(comp.zone)
            assert position_in_zone(result.positions[comp.idx], comp, zone)
    
    # Validate critical loops
    for loop in constraints.critical_loops:
        area = compute_loop_area(result.positions, loop, netlist)
        assert area <= loop.max_area_mm2 * 1.1  # 10% tolerance

# Test 2: Comparison with gradient-based placement
def test_deterministic_vs_gradient_quality():
    '''Compare quality metrics between deterministic and gradient-based.'''
    netlist = load_test_netlist()
    board = load_test_board()
    constraints = load_test_constraints()
    
    # Deterministic placement
    det_placer = DeterministicPlacer()
    det_result = det_placer.place(netlist, board, constraints, DeterministicPlacerConfig())
    
    # Gradient-based placement
    grad_result = run_gradient_placement(netlist, board, constraints)
    
    # Deterministic should be at least 80% as good on wirelength
    det_wl = compute_total_wirelength(det_result.positions, netlist)
    grad_wl = compute_total_wirelength(grad_result.positions, netlist)
    assert det_wl <= grad_wl * 1.25  # Within 25%
    
    # But should be much faster
    assert det_result.time_seconds < grad_result.time_seconds * 0.1

# Test 3: Performance benchmark
def test_deterministic_placement_performance():
    '''Placement should complete within 30 seconds on Temper board.'''
    netlist = load_temper_netlist()  # ~50 components
    board = load_temper_board()
    constraints = load_temper_constraints()
    
    placer = DeterministicPlacer()
    config = DeterministicPlacerConfig()
    
    start = time.time()
    result = placer.place(netlist, board, constraints, config)
    elapsed = time.time() - start
    
    assert elapsed < 30.0
    assert result.success

# Test 4: Robustness to edge cases
@pytest.mark.parametrize('scenario', [
    'minimal_board',      # Just a few components
    'dense_packing',      # Many components, tight zones
    'no_zones',           # No zone constraints
    'all_fixed',          # All components pre-fixed
    'circular_deps',      # Proximity rules forming cycles
])
def test_deterministic_handles_edge_cases(scenario):
    netlist, board, constraints = load_scenario(scenario)
    
    placer = DeterministicPlacer()
    result = placer.place(netlist, board, constraints, DeterministicPlacerConfig())
    
    # Should not crash, should return valid result or clear error
    assert result.success or len(result.errors) > 0
    if result.success:
        assert not has_overlaps(result.positions, netlist)
```

## Test Scenarios to Cover

1. **Temper board** - Full 50+ component power electronics board
2. **Minimal** - 3-5 components, simple constraints
3. **Dense** - Many components in small zones
4. **No zones** - Only overlap and proximity constraints
5. **Fixed heavy** - Most components pre-fixed
6. **Impossible** - Intentionally unsatisfiable constraints (should fail gracefully)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Tests for full Temper board pass
- [ ] Performance under 30 seconds
- [ ] Edge cases handled gracefully
- [ ] Comparison with gradient-based shows reasonable quality
- [ ] CI pipeline includes these tests

title: Placement-Routing Correlation GPBM Loop
description:
## Goal

Establish a systematic workflow to validate that placement optimizer improvements actually translate to better routed boards.

## Problem Statement

The temper-placer has 29 loss functions but no systematic way to prove that minimizing them produces better routed boards. We need:
1. Unified quality metrics combining placement + routing outcomes
2. A composite quality score for easy comparison
3. Correlation analysis to tune loss weights empirically
4. Integration with the GPBM workflow (bd-done)

## Scope

**In scope:**
- Unified placement quality report script
- Composite quality score (0-100)
- Routing metrics in METRICS.md
- GPBM integration (bd-done auto-runs quality check)
- Correlation analysis script
- Loss weight tuning based on data
- Documentation

**Out of scope (separate epic):**
- FreeRouting wrapper fixes (separate router epic)
- New loss function implementations
- Hand-placed reference creation

## Success Criteria

- Single script produces comprehensive quality report (placement + DRC + routing)
- Quality score provides meaningful comparison between placements
- bd-done automatically runs quality check for placer tasks
- Correlation analysis identifies which losses predict routing success
- Loss weight suggestions based on empirical data
- Workflow documented

## Key Files

- scripts/placement_quality_report.py (new)
- temper-placer/src/temper_placer/metrics/quality_score.py (new)
- tools/bd-worktree-helpers.sh (modify bd-done)
- scripts/correlation_analysis.py (new)
- scripts/tune_loss_weights.py (new)
- metrics/METRICS.md (add routing metrics)
- docs/PLACEMENT_QUALITY_VALIDATION.md (new)

title: Create unified placement_quality_report.py
description:
## Purpose

Create a single script that evaluates all quality metrics for a placed PCB, combining placement analysis, DRC, and optionally routing.

## Location

`scripts/placement_quality_report.py`

## CLI Interface

```bash
# Basic usage (placement + DRC only)
python scripts/placement_quality_report.py --pcb temper.kicad_pcb

# With constraints for loss evaluation
python scripts/placement_quality_report.py --pcb temper.kicad_pcb --config constraints.yaml

# With routing (adds 5-15 min)
python scripts/placement_quality_report.py --pcb temper.kicad_pcb --route

# JSON output
python scripts/placement_quality_report.py --pcb temper.kicad_pcb --json --output report.json
```

## Output JSON Structure

```json
{
  "input_file": "temper.kicad_pcb",
  "timestamp": "2025-12-19T15:00:00Z",
  "placement_metrics": {
    "hpwl_mm": 1234.5,
    "overlap_loss": 0.0,
    "boundary_loss": 0.0,
    "hv_clearance_mm": 12.3,
    "thermal_compliance": true,
    "zone_compliance_pct": 95.0,
    "loop_area_gate_drive_mm2": 45.0
  },
  "drc_metrics": {
    "violations": 0,
    "errors": 0,
    "warnings": 2
  },
  "routing_metrics": {
    "completion_pct": 100.0,
    "wirelength_mm": 1567.8,
    "via_count": 42,
    "routing_time_s": 180.5
  },
  "quality_score": 87.5,
  "pass": true
}
```

## Implementation Approach

1. Parse PCB with kiutils to extract component positions
2. If constraints provided, evaluate loss functions (without optimizing)
3. Run `kicad-cli pcb drc` for DRC metrics
4. If --route flag, invoke FreeRouting wrapper
5. Compute composite quality score
6. Output JSON or human-readable report

## Dependencies

- temper_placer.io.kicad_parser
- temper_placer.losses (for evaluation)
- temper_placer.routing.freerouting (for routing)
- temper_placer.metrics.quality_score (new module)
- kicad-cli (system)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Outputs structured JSON with all metrics
- [ ] CLI help and proper error handling
- [ ] Handles missing kicad-cli gracefully

title: Implement composite quality score module
description:
## Purpose

Create a reusable module that computes a composite placement quality score (0-100) from multiple metrics.

## Location

`temper-placer/src/temper_placer/metrics/quality_score.py`

## API Design

```python
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class QualityInputs:
    """Inputs for quality score computation."""
    # Hard constraints (binary)
    drc_violations: int = 0
    overlap_loss: float = 0.0
    boundary_loss: float = 0.0
    
    # Routing (optional)
    routing_completion_pct: float = 100.0
    
    # Efficiency
    hpwl_mm: float = 0.0
    hpwl_target_mm: Optional[float] = None  # If None, skip this component
    
    # Safety/compliance
    hv_clearance_ok: bool = True
    thermal_compliance: bool = True
    zone_compliance_pct: float = 100.0


def compute_quality_score(inputs: QualityInputs) -> float:
    """
    Compute composite placement quality score (0-100).
    
    Weight breakdown:
    - 40 pts: DRC pass (hard gate - 0 if any violations)
    - 20 pts: Routing completion percentage
    - 15 pts: Wirelength efficiency (HPWL vs target)
    - 10 pts: HV clearance compliance
    - 10 pts: Thermal compliance  
    - 5 pts: Zone compliance percentage
    
    Returns:
        Score from 0-100. 
        Interpretation: 0-60=poor, 60-80=acceptable, 80+=good
    """
    score = 0.0
    
    # Hard gate: DRC must pass
    if inputs.drc_violations == 0 and inputs.overlap_loss < 1.0 and inputs.boundary_loss < 1.0:
        score += 40.0
    
    # Routing completion
    score += 20.0 * (inputs.routing_completion_pct / 100.0)
    
    # Wirelength efficiency
    if inputs.hpwl_target_mm and inputs.hpwl_mm > 0:
        efficiency = min(1.0, inputs.hpwl_target_mm / inputs.hpwl_mm)
        score += 15.0 * efficiency
    else:
        score += 15.0  # Full points if not measuring
    
    # Safety compliance
    score += 10.0 if inputs.hv_clearance_ok else 0.0
    score += 10.0 if inputs.thermal_compliance else 0.0
    
    # Zone compliance
    score += 5.0 * (inputs.zone_compliance_pct / 100.0)
    
    return score


def interpret_score(score: float) -> str:
    """Human-readable interpretation."""
    if score >= 80:
        return "good"
    elif score >= 60:
        return "acceptable"
    else:
        return "poor"
```

## Also Create

- `temper-placer/src/temper_placer/metrics/__init__.py` (new package)
- Export `compute_quality_score`, `QualityInputs`, `interpret_score`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] QualityInputs dataclass with all fields
- [ ] compute_quality_score returns 0-100
- [ ] interpret_score provides human-readable output
- [ ] Unit tests cover edge cases (all zeros, perfect score, etc.)
- [ ] Integrated into placement_quality_report.py

title: Integrate quality report into bd-done workflow
description:
## Purpose

Automatically run placement quality checks when completing placer-related tasks via bd-done.

## Location

`tools/bd-worktree-helpers.sh` - modify `bd-done()` function

## Implementation

Add to bd-done() after the measurement phase but before closing:

```bash
# After running bd-measure, before bd close
# Check if this is a placer task and run quality report

_run_placer_quality_check() {
    local task_id="$1"
    
    # Check if task has placer-related labels
    local labels
    labels=$(bd --sandbox show "$task_id" --json 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.labels // [] | join(",")')
    
    if [[ "$labels" == *"placer"* ]] || [[ "$labels" == *"domain:placer"* ]]; then
        echo "Running placement quality check..."
        
        # Find a test PCB in the worktree
        local pcb_file
        pcb_file=$(find . -name "*.kicad_pcb" -not -path "*/backup/*" | head -1)
        
        if [[ -n "$pcb_file" ]] && [[ -f "scripts/placement_quality_report.py" ]]; then
            local report_file="/tmp/quality_report_${task_id}.json"
            
            if python3 scripts/placement_quality_report.py --pcb "$pcb_file" --json --output "$report_file" 2>/dev/null; then
                local score
                score=$(jq -r '.quality_score // 0' "$report_file")
                local interpretation
                interpretation=$(jq -r '.interpretation // "unknown"' "$report_file")
                
                echo "Placement quality score: $score ($interpretation)"
                
                if (( $(echo "$score < 60" | bc -l) )); then
                    echo "⚠️  Warning: Quality score below threshold (60)"
                    echo "   Review: $report_file"
                fi
            else
                echo "Note: Quality report failed (non-blocking)"
            fi
        fi
    fi
}

# Call in bd-done after measurements
_run_placer_quality_check "$task_id"
```

## Behavior

1. Only runs for tasks with `placer` or `domain:placer` labels
2. Finds first .kicad_pcb file in worktree
3. Runs placement_quality_report.py (no routing - too slow)
4. Displays score and interpretation
5. Warns if score < 60 (but doesn't block)
6. Non-blocking on failure (graceful degradation)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Quality check runs automatically for placer tasks
- [ ] Score displayed to user with interpretation
- [ ] Warning shown for scores < 60
- [ ] Graceful handling if no PCB found
- [ ] Graceful handling if script fails
- [ ] Does not block task completion
- [ ] Only runs for placer-labeled tasks

title: Create correlation analysis script
description:
## Purpose

Empirically determine which loss functions actually predict routing success by running batch optimizations and correlating losses with routing outcomes.

## Location

`scripts/correlation_analysis.py`

## CLI Interface

```bash
# Run correlation analysis on a PCB
python scripts/correlation_analysis.py \
    --pcb piantor_left.kicad_pcb \
    --config constraints.yaml \
    --samples 30 \
    --output correlation_report.json

# Quick analysis (fewer samples)
python scripts/correlation_analysis.py \
    --pcb piantor_left.kicad_pcb \
    --samples 10 \
    --quick  # Skip routing, use HPWL as proxy
```

## Algorithm

1. **Setup**: Load PCB and constraints
2. **Batch Optimization**: For i in 1..N samples:
   - Run optimizer with seed=i
   - Record all loss values at convergence
   - Run FreeRouting (or skip with --quick)
   - Record routing metrics
3. **Correlation Analysis**:
   - Compute Pearson correlation between each loss and:
     - routing_completion_pct
     - routed_wirelength_mm
     - via_count
   - Compute Spearman (rank) correlation for robustness
4. **Generate Recommendations**:
   - High |r| (>0.7): "Keep/increase weight"
   - Low |r| (<0.3): "Consider reducing weight"
   - Negative r with completion: "This loss helps routing"

## Output JSON Structure

```json
{
  "pcb": "piantor_left.kicad_pcb",
  "n_samples": 30,
  "routing_mode": "freerouting",
  "correlations": {
    "wirelength_loss": {
      "vs_completion": 0.15,
      "vs_routed_wirelength": 0.92,
      "vs_via_count": 0.45
    },
    "overlap_loss": {
      "vs_completion": -0.85,
      "vs_routed_wirelength": 0.23,
      "vs_via_count": 0.12
    },
    "congestion_loss": {
      "vs_completion": -0.65,
      "vs_routed_wirelength": 0.55,
      "vs_via_count": 0.78
    },
    "thermal_loss": {
      "vs_completion": 0.08,
      "vs_routed_wirelength": 0.12,
      "vs_via_count": -0.05
    }
  },
  "recommendations": [
    {
      "loss": "wirelength_loss",
      "action": "keep",
      "reason": "Strong correlation with routed wirelength (r=0.92)"
    },
    {
      "loss": "overlap_loss", 
      "action": "increase",
      "reason": "Strong negative correlation with completion (r=-0.85) - overlaps block routing"
    },
    {
      "loss": "thermal_loss",
      "action": "review",
      "reason": "Weak correlation with routing metrics - may not impact routability"
    }
  ],
  "statistics": {
    "mean_completion_pct": 98.5,
    "std_completion_pct": 3.2,
    "failed_routes": 2
  }
}
```

## Implementation Notes

- Use multiprocessing to parallelize optimization runs
- Cache routing results (routing is slow)
- Handle FreeRouting failures gracefully
- Use scipy.stats for correlation computation

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Runs batch of optimizations with varied seeds
- [ ] Routes all placements (can be slow)
- [ ] Computes Pearson and Spearman correlations
- [ ] Generates actionable recommendations
- [ ] Works with any PCB + constraints combo
- [ ] --quick mode for fast iteration (no routing)
- [ ] Handles routing failures gracefully
- [ ] JSON output for downstream processing

## Estimated Runtime

- 30 samples x (60s optimization + 300s routing) = ~3 hours
- With --quick (no routing): 30 samples x 60s = ~30 minutes

title: Create loss weight tuning script
description:
## Purpose

Use correlation analysis data to suggest improved loss weights for the optimizer.

## Location

`scripts/tune_loss_weights.py`

## CLI Interface

```bash
# Generate tuned weights from correlation data
python scripts/tune_loss_weights.py \
    --correlation-report correlation_report.json \
    --base-config configs/temper_constraints.yaml \
    --output configs/temper_constraints_tuned.yaml

# Preview changes without writing
python scripts/tune_loss_weights.py \
    --correlation-report correlation_report.json \
    --base-config configs/temper_constraints.yaml \
    --dry-run
```

## Algorithm

1. Load correlation report (from correlation_analysis.py)
2. Load base constraint config with current weights
3. For each loss term:
   - If |r_completion| > 0.7: Scale weight by 1.5x
   - If |r_completion| > 0.5: Keep current weight
   - If |r_completion| < 0.3: Scale weight by 0.5x
   - Never reduce hard constraint weights (overlap, boundary, clearance)
4. Normalize weights to maintain relative scale
5. Output updated YAML with comments explaining changes

## Output YAML Example

```yaml
# Auto-tuned weights based on correlation analysis
# Generated: 2025-12-19T15:00:00Z
# Source: correlation_report.json

losses:
  # HARD CONSTRAINTS (never reduced)
  overlap:
    weight: 1000  # Unchanged - hard constraint
  boundary:
    weight: 1000  # Unchanged - hard constraint
  clearance:
    weight: 1000  # Unchanged - hard constraint
  
  # TUNED WEIGHTS
  wirelength:
    weight: 1.5   # Increased 1.5x: r=0.92 with routed wirelength
    # Original: 1.0
  
  congestion:
    weight: 7.5   # Increased 1.5x: r=-0.65 with completion
    # Original: 5.0
  
  thermal:
    weight: 2.5   # Reduced 0.5x: r=0.08 with completion (weak)
    # Original: 5.0
    # NOTE: Review if thermal is important for non-routing reasons
  
  loop_area:
    weight: 5.0   # Unchanged: r=0.45 (moderate)
    # Original: 5.0
```

## Safety Rules

1. **Never reduce hard constraints**: overlap, boundary, clearance, zone_membership
2. **Cap multipliers**: 0.25x minimum, 4x maximum
3. **Preserve user overrides**: If config has `# USER: keep` comment, don't change
4. **Log all changes**: Output reasoning for each adjustment

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Reads correlation analysis JSON
- [ ] Produces valid constraints YAML
- [ ] Documents reasoning for each change in comments
- [ ] Never reduces hard constraint weights
- [ ] --dry-run shows changes without writing
- [ ] Validates output config is syntactically valid
- [ ] Unit tests for weight adjustment logic

title: Connect LayerStackup to MazeRouter for layer-aware routing
description:

2. Check is_routable flag in _get_neighbors() - skip power/ground planes
3. Add route_net() method that respects net_class layer restrictions
4. Use routable_layers(net_class) to get allowed layers per net
5. Add tests for routing on signal layers only

Current state:
- LayerStackup.default_4layer() defines L2=GND, L3=PWR as is_routable=False

- Preserve ground plane integrity (critical for EMI)
- Route HV traces only on L1 (2oz copper)
- Proper layer stackup compliance

title: Training loop and convergence verification
description: Training loop has edge cases: rotation logits not informed by placement, virtual node margin can exceed small boards, convergence velocity can return NaN.
acceptance_criteria:
- Rotation logits initialized from placement geometry
- Virtual node margin validated against board size
- Convergence velocity handles zero-variance windows
- Plateau detection has no race conditions
close_reason: Closed

title: Convergence velocity NaN prevention
description: Velocity calculation uses linear regression on improvement rate window. Division by denominator could be zero if window has zero variance (all rates identical).
acceptance_criteria:
- Zero variance → return 0.0 velocity (not NaN)
- Add explicit variance check before division
- Test: 10 identical improvement rates → velocity = 0
- No NaN propagation to early stopping logic
close_reason: Closed

title: Virtual node margin vs small board validation
description: Virtual nodes initialized with margin=10mm from board edge. If board is very small (<20mm), interior is empty or negative.
acceptance_criteria:
- Margin validated against board size
- Small boards: margin clamped to board_size/4
- Test: 15mm board → margin reduced automatically
- Error if board too small for any virtual nodes
close_reason: Closed

title: Rotation logits initialization from placement
description: All components start with uniform rotation distribution (zeros). Should be informed by initial placement, component aspect ratio, or board geometry.
acceptance_criteria:
- Tall components on tall board → bias toward 0°/180°
- Wide components → bias toward 90°/270°
- Or: inherit rotation from initial placement heuristic
- Test: 1x10mm component on 5x50mm board → non-uniform logits
close_reason: Closed

title: Plateau detection race condition
description: If improvement stays exactly at threshold, plateau_event can be created twice. Line 231 creates event but doesn't add to list atomically.
acceptance_criteria:
- Plateau event created exactly once per plateau
- Edge case: improvement == threshold exactly → handled
- Test: improvement oscillates around threshold → single event
close_reason: Closed

title: Stagnation threshold configurability
description: Hardcoded stagnation threshold 0.0001. For small boards or low-loss problems, legitimate improvements < 0.01% are treated as stagnation.
acceptance_criteria:
- Stagnation threshold configurable in TrainingConfig
- Default remains 0.0001 for backward compatibility
- Document when to adjust threshold
- Test: tiny board with sub-threshold improvements → not stagnant
close_reason: Closed

description: Improve fanout_power.py to compute better via placement (center of zones, not edges) and update add_power_planes_v2.py to create full-board zones instead of bounding-box zones. This should resolve the remaining 34 unconnected items.

description:

- power_zone: [0, 50, 100, 80] - 30mm height
- driver_zone: [0, 30, 100, 50] - 20mm height  
- control_zone: [0, 0, 100, 30] - 30mm height
- interface_zone: [0, 70, 15, 80] - 10mm height

## Components to Check
- IGBTs (Q1, Q2) - large TO-247 packages
- Bus caps (C_BUS1, C_BUS2) - large radial caps
- MCU (U_MCU) - QFN-56
- Gate driver (U_GATE) - SOIC-16

## Acceptance Criteria
- All components fit in assigned zones with margin
- Zone assignments are feasible
- Documentation of any conflicts found

title: Monorepo Restructure: uv Workspaces for Python Packages
description:
## Goal

Restructure the Python codebase into proper uv workspaces with multiple packages, eliminating sys.path hacks and enabling independent versioning.

## Background

Current state has fragmented Python code:
- `temper-placer/` - Proper package with pyproject.toml
- `scripts/` - Loose scripts with sys.path.append hacks
- `tools/gpbm/` - Has __init__.py but no pyproject.toml
- `tools/agents/` - Loose scripts
- `tools/*.py` - One-off utilities

Problems:
1. sys.path.append hacks are fragile
2. Two 'scripts' directories (root and temper-placer/)
3. tools/gpbm is a pseudo-package without proper packaging
4. No unified dependency management
5. Overlapping concerns between scripts/ and temper-placer/

## Target Structure

```
temper/
├── pyproject.toml            # Root workspace config
├── packages/
│   ├── temper-placer/        # PCB placement optimizer (existing)
│   ├── temper-workflow/      # GPBM + routing + metrics orchestration (NEW)
│   └── temper-tools/         # One-off utilities (NEW)
├── firmware/                 # Unchanged
├── elec/                     # Unchanged
└── pcb/                      # Unchanged
```

## Success Criteria

- [ ] All Python code installable via `uv sync`
- [ ] No sys.path.append hacks
- [ ] `temper-placer` remains independently publishable to PyPI
- [ ] All existing scripts work via CLI entry points
- [ ] Tests pass in CI with workspace install

## Key Files

- temper-placer/pyproject.toml (existing)
- scripts/*.py (to migrate)
- tools/gpbm/*.py (to migrate)
- tools/agents/*.py (to migrate)
- tools/*.py (to migrate)

title: Create root pyproject.toml with uv workspace config
description:
## Task

Create the root workspace configuration file that defines all member packages.

## Implementation

Create `pyproject.toml` at repo root:

```toml
[project]
name = "temper-workspace"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "Temper induction cooker development workspace"
requires-python = ">=3.11"

[tool.uv]
dev-dependencies = [
    "pytest>=7.4.0",
    "ruff>=0.1.6",
    "mypy>=1.7.0",
]

[tool.uv.workspace]
members = ["packages/*"]

[tool.uv.sources]
temper-placer = { workspace = true }
temper-workflow = { workspace = true }
temper-tools = { workspace = true }
```

## Verification

- `uv sync` completes without errors
- All packages appear in `.venv/lib/python3.*/site-packages/`

## Files

- pyproject.toml (new)

```bash
mkdir -p packages
git mv temper-placer packages/temper-placer
```

title: Create temper-workflow package from tools/gpbm and scripts/
description:
## Task

Create a new temper-workflow package that consolidates GPBM workflow tools and routing/metrics scripts.

## Package Structure

```
packages/temper-workflow/
├── pyproject.toml
├── src/
│   └── temper_workflow/
│       ├── __init__.py
│       ├── gpbm/              # From tools/gpbm/
│       │   ├── __init__.py
│       │   ├── gather.py
│       │   ├── plan.py
│       │   ├── measure.py
│       │   ├── eco_client.py
│       │   └── requirements_parser.py
│       ├── routing/           # From scripts/
│       │   ├── __init__.py
│       │   ├── route_and_measure.py
│       │   └── steiner_sweep.py
│       ├── metrics/           # From scripts/
│       │   ├── __init__.py
│       │   ├── measure_displacement.py
│       │   ├── aesthetic_turing_test.py
│       │   └── compare_refinement.py
│       └── agents/            # From tools/agents/
│           ├── __init__.py
│           ├── assign.py
│           ├── auto_assign.py
│           └── dispatch_core.sh (keep as data file)
└── tests/
    └── test_gpbm.py
```

## pyproject.toml

```toml
[project]
name = "temper-workflow"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "GPBM workflow orchestration for Temper development"
requires-python = ">=3.11"
dependencies = [
    "temper-placer",  # workspace dependency
    "pyyaml>=6.0",
    "requests>=2.28.0",
    "rich>=13.0.0",
]

[project.scripts]
temper-gather = "temper_workflow.gpbm.gather:main"
temper-plan = "temper_workflow.gpbm.plan:main"
temper-measure = "temper_workflow.gpbm.measure:main"
temper-route-measure = "temper_workflow.routing.route_and_measure:main"
temper-aesthetic = "temper_workflow.metrics.aesthetic_turing_test:main"
```

## Migration Steps

1. Create directory structure
2. Move files with git mv
3. Update all imports to use temper_workflow.* paths
4. Remove sys.path.append hacks
5. Add proper __init__.py files
6. Create pyproject.toml with dependencies

## Verification

- `uv pip install -e packages/temper-workflow` works
- All CLI entry points work
- Imports of temper_placer work without sys.path hacks
- GPBM workflow functions correctly

## Files

- packages/temper-workflow/ (new)
- tools/gpbm/ (remove after migration)
- scripts/*.py (remove after migration)

title: Add CI workflow for workspace testing
description:
## Task

Create or update CI configuration to test the workspace structure.

## Implementation

Create `.github/workflows/test.yml` (or update existing):

```yaml
name: Test

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Install uv
        uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v4
        with:
          version: "latest"
      
      - name: Set up Python
        run: uv python install 3.12
      
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: uv sync --all-packages
      
      - name: Run temper-placer tests
        run: uv run pytest packages/temper-placer/tests -v
      
      - name: Run temper-workflow tests
        run: uv run pytest packages/temper-workflow/tests -v
      
      - name: Type check
        run: |
          uv run mypy packages/temper-placer/src
          uv run mypy packages/temper-workflow/src
      
      - name: Lint
        run: uv run ruff check packages/
```

## Verification

- CI passes on test branch before merging
- All packages are tested
- Type checking and linting run

## Files

- .github/workflows/test.yml (new or update)

title: Create XC6220 KiCad symbol
description: The XC6220B331MR-G 3.3V LDO was selected in sim_15 but has no KiCad symbol. Need to create SOT-25 (SC-74A) footprint symbol for schematic capture. This is blocking PCB layout.

title: NSGA-II Correctness and Performance Fixes
description: Address critical bugs, design issues, and performance bottlenecks in the NSGA-II multi-objective optimizer implementation. All fixes must be test-driven with clear oracles.

title: CRITICAL: Fix odd population size crash in crossover
description:
**Bug**: When population_size is odd, parent pairing crashes due to shape mismatch.

**Location**: nsga2.py:299-305
```python
p1_idx = parent_indices[::2]  # length 25 if pop_size=50
p2_idx = parent_indices[1::2]  # length 25 if pop_size=50
```

**Oracle/Test**: Create test that runs evolve() with population_size=51 and verifies no crash.

**Root Cause**: Slicing parent_indices[::2] and parent_indices[1::2] produces different lengths when pop_size is odd.

**Fix Strategy**: 
1. Ensure even number of parents selected for crossover
2. Handle remaining individual separately

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- Test with pop_size=51 passes
- Test with pop_size=49 passes  
- All existing tests still pass

title: Document NSGA-II algorithm and usage guide
description:
**Issue**: NSGA-II implementation lacks comprehensive documentation for users.

**Deliverables**:

**1. Algorithm Documentation** (nsga2.py docstrings)
- Mathematical formulation of domination
- Fast non-dominated sorting complexity analysis
- Crowding distance intuition and formula
- Tournament selection operator
- Crossover and mutation strategies

**2. Usage Guide** (docs/optimizer/NSGA2_GUIDE.md)
- When to use NSGA-II vs gradient descent
- How to define objectives
- How to interpret Pareto fronts
- How to select from Pareto solutions
- Parameter tuning guidelines (pop_size, generations, mutation_rate)

**3. Examples**
- Multi-objective placement example with wirelength vs thermal
- Visualization of Pareto front evolution
- Comparing NSGA-II vs weighted sum approach

**4. API Reference**
- NSGAOptimizer parameters and their effects
- NSGAResult structure
- Plotting utilities (plot_pareto_front)

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- All public functions have comprehensive docstrings
- NSGA2_GUIDE.md created with examples
- Code examples run without errors
- Sphinx documentation builds successfully
- At least one Jupyter notebook tutorial

title: CRITICAL: Fix O(N²×F) rank assignment performance
description:
**Bug**: Rank assignment has O(N × F × |F|) complexity, causing severe slowdown with large populations.

**Location**: nsga2.py:291-292
```python
jnp.array([next(i for i, f in enumerate(fronts) if idx in f) 
           for idx in range(self.pop_size)])
```

**Oracle/Test**: 
1. Benchmark test: evolve() with pop_size=200 should complete <5 seconds
2. Unit test: verify rank assignment matches fronts

**Root Cause**: Searches all fronts for each individual instead of building ranks during front construction.

**Fix Strategy**: Build rank array in O(N) while constructing fronts:
```python
ranks = np.zeros(n, dtype=int)
for rank_val, front in enumerate(fronts):
    for idx in front:
        ranks[idx] = rank_val
```

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- Rank assignment is O(N)
- Benchmark with pop_size=200 passes
- Correctness test verifies ranks match fronts
- All existing tests pass
close_reason: Optimized rank assignment to O(N) using numpy. Fixed tests.

title: Return Pareto front instead of scalarized solution
description:
**Issue**: NsgaPhase.run() defeats multi-objective purpose by summing objectives to pick single solution.

**Location**: phases.py:190
```python
best_idx = int(jnp.argmin(jnp.sum(result.objectives, axis=1)))
```

**Oracle/Test**:
1. Integration test: verify NsgaPhase returns full Pareto front
2. Test that all solutions in returned front are non-dominated
3. Test that front contains >1 solution for multi-objective problems

**Problem**: NSGA-II finds Pareto-optimal trade-offs, but pipeline throws away all but one solution.

**Fix Strategy**: 
1. Return entire Pareto front (result.best_indices) 
2. Implement knee-point selection as default heuristic
3. Allow user to specify objective preferences
4. Update OptimizationPipeline to handle multiple solutions

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- NsgaPhase returns ParetoFrontResult with all non-dominated solutions
- Knee-point selection test validates geometric heuristic
- Integration test verifies Pareto front preservation through pipeline
- Documentation explains how to select from Pareto front

title: Implement rotation crossover and mutation
description:
**Issue**: Rotations not evolved - only copied from parent, limiting search space.

**Location**: nsga2.py:260-265, 320
```python
# Init: clones rotation without perturbation
pop_rot = jnp.repeat(initial_state.rotation_logits[None, :, :], self.pop_size, axis=0)

# Crossover: copies from parent 1, doesn't blend
child_rot = pop_rot[p1_idx]
```

**Oracle/Test**:
1. Rotation diversity test: verify rotation variance increases over generations
2. Crossover test: verify child rotations differ from both parents
3. Mutation test: verify rotation_logits change with mutation rate

**Problem**: Rotations frozen, preventing exploration of rotation configurations.

**Fix Strategy**:
1. Add rotation perturbation in initialization
2. Implement rotation crossover (blend logits with BLX-alpha)
3. Implement rotation mutation (Gaussian noise on logits)

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- Rotation variance test passes (variance > initial after N generations)
- Crossover produces different rotations from parents
- Mutation test verifies stochastic rotation changes
- Integration test shows rotation optimization on rotation-sensitive problem
close_reason: Implemented rotation crossover and mutation with BLX-alpha blending and Gaussian noise

title: Vectorize domination checks with vmap
description:
**Issue**: Domination checks use Python loops, missing JAX optimization opportunities.

**Location**: nsga2.py:41-57
```python
for i in range(n):
    for j in range(i + 1, n):
        diff = objectives[i] - objectives[j]
        i_dominates_j = jnp.all(diff <= 0) and jnp.any(diff < 0)
```

**Oracle/Test**:
1. Benchmark: fast_non_dominated_sort() with N=1000 should complete <100ms
2. Correctness test: verify vectorized version produces identical fronts
3. Property test: verify on randomly generated objective matrices

**Problem**: O(N²) loop not vectorized, leaving performance on table.

**Fix Strategy**:
1. Use vmap for inner loop over j  
2. Consider full vectorization with triangle mask
3. Balance readability vs performance

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- Benchmark test passes (<100ms for N=1000)
- Correctness test: vectorized == original on test cases
- Property test: 100 random matrices produce identical results
- Code maintains readability

title: Lazy crowding distance for partial front selection
description:
**Issue**: Crowding distance computed for all 2N individuals but only used for last partial front.

**Location**: nsga2.py:331
```python
combined_distances = calculate_crowding_distance(combined_obj)
# Only used in loop when filling last front
```

**Oracle/Test**:
1. Benchmark: evolve() with pop_size=100 should be ≥10% faster
2. Correctness test: verify same individuals selected with lazy computation
3. Memory test: verify reduced memory allocation

**Problem**: Wastes computation on individuals already selected by rank.

**Fix Strategy**:
1. Only compute crowding distance for fronts being considered
2. Compute lazily for last partially-included front
3. Skip computation for fully-included fronts

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- Benchmark shows ≥10% speedup on pop_size=100
- Correctness test: produces identical selection
- Memory profiling shows reduced allocation
- Code clarity maintained

title: Add test coverage for missing edge cases
description:
**Issue**: Missing test coverage for critical edge cases and properties.

**Missing Tests**:
1. ❌ Odd population size (would catch bug #1)
2. ❌ Rotation diversity over generations
3. ❌ Pareto front non-dominance property
4. ❌ Convergence to known Pareto fronts (ZDT test problems)

**Oracle/Test Suite**:

**Test 1: Odd Population Size**
```python
def test_odd_population_size():
    optimizer = NSGAOptimizer(population_size=51)
    result = optimizer.evolve(netlist, board, objectives, context, generations=5)
    assert result.population_positions.shape[0] == 51
```

**Test 2: Rotation Diversity**
```python
def test_rotation_diversity_increases():
    # After N generations, rotation variance should exceed initial
    initial_var = rotation_variance(result.population_rotations, gen=0)
    final_var = rotation_variance(result.population_rotations, gen=50)
    assert final_var > initial_var
```

**Test 3: Pareto Front Non-Dominance**
```python
def test_pareto_front_non_dominated():
    # No solution in front should dominate another
    for i in front:
        for j in front:
            if i != j:
                assert not dominates(objectives[i], objectives[j])
```

**Test 4: ZDT Benchmark Convergence**
```python
def test_zdt1_convergence():
    # NSGA-II should find known Pareto front for ZDT1
    result = run_nsga_on_zdt1()
    pareto_objs = result.objectives[result.best_indices]
    # Verify hypervolume or inverted generational distance
    assert hypervolume(pareto_objs, reference) > 0.99
```

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- All 4 test suites implemented
- Tests added to test_nsga2.py
- CI runs tests on every commit
- All tests pass

title: Implement ZDT test problems as correctness oracles
description:
**Issue**: No ground-truth benchmarks to verify NSGA-II finds correct Pareto fronts.

**Oracle**: ZDT (Zitzler-Deb-Thiele) test suite provides known Pareto fronts for validation.

**Test Problems to Implement**:

**ZDT1**: Convex Pareto front
```python
def zdt1(x):
    f1 = x[0]
    g = 1 + 9 * jnp.mean(x[1:])
    f2 = g * (1 - jnp.sqrt(f1 / g))
    return jnp.array([f1, f2])
# True Pareto front: f2 = 1 - sqrt(f1), f1 ∈ [0,1]
```

**ZDT2**: Non-convex Pareto front
```python
def zdt2(x):
    f1 = x[0]
    g = 1 + 9 * jnp.mean(x[1:])
    f2 = g * (1 - (f1 / g)**2)
    return jnp.array([f1, f2])
```

**ZDT3**: Disconnected Pareto front
```python
def zdt3(x):
    f1 = x[0]
    g = 1 + 9 * jnp.mean(x[1:])
    f2 = g * (1 - jnp.sqrt(f1/g) - (f1/g)*jnp.sin(10*jnp.pi*f1))
    return jnp.array([f1, f2])
```

**Metrics**:
1. **Hypervolume**: Volume dominated by Pareto front
2. **Inverted Generational Distance (IGD)**: Distance to true Pareto front
3. **Spacing**: Uniformity of solutions

**Test Structure**:
```python
def test_nsga2_zdt1():
    result = run_nsga_on_zdt1(generations=100, pop_size=100)
    hv = hypervolume(result.pareto_front, reference_point=[1.1, 1.1])
    assert hv > 0.65  # Known good value
    
def test_nsga2_zdt2():
    # Similar structure
    
def test_nsga2_convergence_monotonic():
    # HV should increase over generations
```

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- ZDT1, ZDT2, ZDT3 test problems implemented
- Hypervolume metric implemented
- IGD metric implemented
- Tests verify NSGA-II achieves >90% of known optimal hypervolume
- Visualization of found vs true Pareto front

title: Property-based tests for domination and sorting
description:
**Issue**: Domination logic only tested on hand-crafted cases, missing edge cases.

**Oracle**: Mathematical properties that must hold for any input.

**Properties to Test**:

**Property 1: Reflexivity**
```python
@given(st.lists(st.floats(min_value=0, max_value=100), min_size=2, max_size=10))
def test_no_solution_dominates_itself(objectives):
    obj = jnp.array([objectives])
    fronts = fast_non_dominated_sort(obj)
    assert fronts == [[0]]  # Single solution in front 0
```

**Property 2: Transitivity**
```python
def test_domination_transitivity():
    # If A dom B and B dom C, then A dom C
    # Generate via hypothesis
```

**Property 3: Front Monotonicity**
```python
def test_front_rank_monotonicity(objectives):
    # All solutions in front[i] should dominate all in front[i+1]
    fronts = fast_non_dominated_sort(objectives)
    for i, front_i in enumerate(fronts[:-1]):
        for j, front_j in enumerate(fronts[i+1:], start=i+1):
            for sol_i in front_i:
                # sol_i should dominate at least one in front_j
                # or be incomparable to all
```

**Property 4: Front Completeness**
```python
def test_all_individuals_in_fronts(objectives):
    n = objectives.shape[0]
    fronts = fast_non_dominated_sort(objectives)
    all_indices = set()
    for front in fronts:
        all_indices.update(front)
    assert all_indices == set(range(n))
```

**Property 5: Crowding Distance Bounds**
```python
def test_crowding_distance_extremes_infinite(objectives):
    # Extremes in each objective should have infinite distance
    distances = calculate_crowding_distance(objectives)
    for obj_idx in range(objectives.shape[1]):
        min_idx = jnp.argmin(objectives[:, obj_idx])
        max_idx = jnp.argmax(objectives[:, obj_idx])
        assert distances[min_idx] == float('inf')
        assert distances[max_idx] == float('inf')
```

**Implementation**:
- Use `hypothesis` for property-based testing
- Generate random objective matrices
- Test with varying dimensions (2-10 objectives)
- Test with varying population sizes (1-500)

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- All 5 properties implemented with hypothesis
- Tests run 100+ random cases each
- No failures on random inputs
- Integration with pytest

title: Fix hypothesis fixture warnings in push-shove tests

6 tests in push_shove/ are failing due to hypothesis HealthCheck warnings about function-scoped fixtures. Need to create grids inline instead of using fixtures with @given() decorated tests.

Files affected:

In optimizer/validation_callback.py line 351, the SPICE validation integration is just a placeholder:

title: Thermal spreading constraints for heat distribution
description:
Add thermal spreading model to prevent hot spots and ensure even heat distribution.

## Background
High-power components (Q1, Q2, D1, D2) dissipate 20-50W each. Need to model heat spreading to:
- Prevent clustering of hot components
- Ensure adequate spacing for thermal management
- Guide placement toward board edges (heatsink mounting)

## Implementation
1. Thermal resistance network model
2. Heat spreading from component to board edge
3. Temperature rise calculation
4. Constraint: max temp rise < 20°C

## Config Format
```yaml
thermal_properties:
  high_power:
    components: [Q1, Q2]
    power_dissipation_w: {Q1: 30, Q2: 30}
    min_separation_mm: 15.0
  heat_sensitive:
    components: [U_MCU]
    max_temp_rise_c: 20.0
```

## Files
- src/temper_placer/physics/thermal.py (new)
- src/temper_placer/losses/thermal_spreading.py (new)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Hot components separated by >15mm
- Heat-sensitive components >20mm from hot spots
- Thermal pads prefer board edges

## Priority
Long-term (requires thermal modeling)

title: Epic: Eco Memory Browser Frontend Improvements
description:
# Eco Memory Browser Frontend Improvements

## Background
The Eco Memory Browser at `eco.bennetleff.workers.dev` has a basic interface (User ID input + Search/Load All + table display). This epic tracks improvements to make it more useful for browsing and managing semantic memories.

## Current State
- Simple HTML table view
- Manual User ID input (no presets)
- No filtering, sorting, or faceting
- No score visualization
- No multi-namespace search

## Goals
1. Improve search UX with namespace presets and controls
2. Better results display with cards, scores, and previews
3. Add filtering and faceting capabilities
4. Memory details panel for inspection
5. Analytics dashboard for memory insights
6. Agent integration features (token counting, GATHER preview)

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Can search across multiple namespaces with one query
- [ ] Can filter by tags, sectors, and date ranges
- [ ] Score visualization helps identify relevance at a glance
- [ ] Token counter helps estimate context cost
- [ ] Card-based view is easier to scan than table

## Related
- temper-8k1 (Memory Token Cost Experiment) - established baseline metrics
- tools/gpbm/eco_client.py - Python client with search_comprehensive()
close_reason: Successfully completed all frontend improvements for the Eco Memory Browser. Added namespace presets, score thresholding, card-based results view, tag filtering sidebar, analytics dashboard, token counter, and GATHER preview simulator.

title: Add namespace dropdown with presets
description:
## Problem
Users must manually type User IDs like `temper-agent` or `temper-shared`.

## Solution
Add a dropdown/select with common namespaces:
- `temper-agent` (Legacy - where most data lives)
- `temper-shared` (Shared knowledge)
- `temper-architect`, `temper-coder`, `temper-tester` (Roles)
- `temper-firmware`, `temper-placer`, `temper-pcb` (Domains)

## Implementation
- Replace text input with <select> dropdown
- Keep "Custom..." option that shows text input
- Default to `temper-agent` (most memories)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Dropdown shows all standard namespaces
- [ ] Can still enter custom User ID
- [ ] Selected namespace persists in URL/localStorage

title: Add GATHER preview simulator
description:
## Problem
Hard to understand what memories GPBM GATHER will inject for a given query.

## Solution
Add a "GATHER Preview" mode that simulates what gather.py would return:
- Input: goal, domain, role
- Output: simulated GATHER results with token count
- Matches gather.py behavior (min_score, limit, namespace selection)

## Implementation
- New tab/mode: "GATHER Preview"
- Form with goal, domain dropdown, role dropdown
- Shows results exactly as GATHER would format them
- Displays total token count

## Reference
See tools/gpbm/gather.py `_search_eco()` for exact behavior:
- min_score: 0.6 (or ECO_MIN_SCORE env var)
- limit: 5 (or ECO_LIMIT env var)
- Searches legacy namespace + role/domain namespaces

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Can enter goal/domain/role
- [ ] Results match gather.py output
- [ ] Token count displayed
- [ ] Can adjust min_score and limit
close_reason: Implemented GATHER preview simulator. Allows users to query memories using goal, role, and domain parameters and see simulated GPBM GATHER output with token counts. Verified in docs/eco_browser/test_card_view.html.

title: Add score threshold slider
description:
## Problem
No way to adjust minimum similarity score threshold in the UI.

## Solution
Add a slider control for `minScore` parameter:
- Range: 0.5 to 1.0
- Default: 0.7
- Step: 0.05
- Show current value next to slider

## Implementation
- Add range input below search box
- Update search API call to include minScore
- Persist preference in localStorage

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Slider adjusts minScore from 0.5-1.0
- [ ] Current value displayed
- [ ] Search results update when threshold changes

title: Add content truncation with expand
description:
## Problem
Long memory content makes the table hard to scan.

## Solution
Truncate content to ~200 characters with "Show more" expand button.

## Implementation
- Truncate at 200 chars (or nearest word boundary)
- Add "..." and "Show more" link
- Clicking expands to full content in-place
- "Show less" collapses back

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Long content truncated with ellipsis
- [ ] Expand/collapse toggle works
- [ ] Preserves markdown formatting when expanded

title: Add score visualization (color bar)
description:
## Problem
Score values like "0.78" are hard to interpret at a glance.

## Solution
Add colored progress bar showing score visually:
- Red: < 0.7 (low relevance)
- Yellow: 0.7 - 0.85 (medium relevance)
- Green: > 0.85 (high relevance)

## Implementation
- Replace or augment numeric score with colored bar
- Bar width proportional to score (0.5-1.0 range)
- Show numeric value on hover

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Score shown as colored bar
- [ ] Color indicates relevance tier
- [ ] Numeric score visible on hover/click
close_reason: Closed

title: Add multi-namespace search with checkboxes
description:
## Problem
Can only search one namespace at a time in UI, but CLI supports comprehensive search across multiple namespaces.

## Solution
Add checkboxes to select multiple namespaces for a single search (like `search_comprehensive()` in eco_client.py).

## Implementation
- Add checkbox group below namespace dropdown
- Presets: "Legacy (temper-agent)", "Shared", "All Roles", "All Domains"
- Deduplicate results by contentHash (keep highest score)
- Show source namespace badge on each result

## API Changes
Backend may need to support multiple userIds in single request, OR frontend makes parallel requests and merges.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Can select multiple namespaces
- [ ] Results deduplicated by content
- [ ] Source namespace shown on each result
close_reason: Implemented multi-namespace selection with checkboxes and updated filtering logic. Verified in docs/eco_browser/test_card_view.html.

title: Add tag filtering sidebar
description:
## Problem
No way to filter by tags in the UI.

## Solution
Add a sidebar/panel with clickable tag chips for filtering:
- Extract all unique tags from loaded memories
- Show as clickable chips with counts
- Clicking a tag filters to memories with that tag
- Support multiple tag selection (AND logic)

## Common Tags
- `role:architect`, `role:coder`, `role:tester`
- `domain:firmware`, `domain:placer`, `domain:pcb`
- `beads`, `reflection`, `issue`, `epic`

## Implementation
- Parse tags from all loaded memories
- Display as chips with frequency count
- Filter client-side for instant response
- "Clear filters" button to reset

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Tags displayed as clickable chips
- [ ] Click filters results to matching memories
- [ ] Multiple tags can be selected
- [ ] Clear all filters button works
close_reason: Implemented tag filtering sidebar with dynamic counts and active state. Verified in docs/eco_browser/test_card_view.html.

title: Add card-based view for results
description:
## Problem
Table view is hard to scan for long content and metadata.

## Solution
Replace table with card-based layout:
